items.q: When you reverse a reaction in a Hess cycle, what happens to ΔH?
items.correct: Its sign reverses — if the forward reaction is +100 kJ/mol, the reverse is -100 kJ/mol.
items.misconception: ΔH stays the same because it's the same reaction.
items.random_wrong: ΔH becomes zero because the change cancels out.
items.why: Students treat ΔH as a property of the reactants/products rather than a directed quantity that flips with direction.

items.q: What kind of bond forms between a ligand and a central metal ion in a complex?
items.correct: A dative (coordinate) covalent bond — the ligand donates a lone pair into an empty orbital on the metal.
items.misconception: The ligand forms an ionic bond with the metal because the metal is positively charged.
items.random_wrong: The ligand shares electrons equally with the metal as a normal covalent bond.
items.why: Students see the +ve metal and assume electrostatic ionic bonding, missing that ligands donate lone pairs into vacant d-orbitals.

items.q: Why can an endothermic reaction still be spontaneous?
items.correct: If the entropy increase (TΔS) is large enough to outweigh the positive ΔH, ΔG can still be negative.
items.misconception: Endothermic reactions are never spontaneous because they require energy input.
items.random_wrong: All endothermic reactions are spontaneous at room temperature.
items.why: Students equate 'exothermic' with 'spontaneous' from GCSE, missing the role of entropy in Gibbs free energy.

items.q: Hess's law states ΔH depends only on:
items.why: It's Initial and final states. “Path taken” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Bond enthalpies refer to bonds broken in which state?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Gas. “Solid” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Bonds broken: 1500 kJ. Bonds made: 1700 kJ. ΔH in kJ/mol is:
items.why: The real figure is -200. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
